topics.title: Data Types & Variables
topics.description: Understand Python's core data types: int, float, str, bool.

lessons.title: What are data types?
lessons.body: Every value in Python has a type. The type determines what operations you can do with it and how it's stored in memory. Python figures out the type automatically — you don't need to declare it.

lessons.title: Integers and floats
lessons.body: Integers (int) are whole numbers like 1, -5, 100. Floats have decimal points like 3.14, -0.5. Dividing two integers with / always gives a float in Python 3.
lessons.code:
age = 25
pi = 3.14159
print(type(age))  # <class 'int'>
print(type(pi))   # <class 'float'>

lessons.title: Strings
lessons.body: Strings store text. Use single or double quotes — both work. You can even use triple quotes for multi-line strings.
lessons.code:
name = "Alice"
greeting = 'Hello'
print(name[0])  # 'A'

lessons.title: Booleans
lessons.body: Booleans are either True or False. They're the result of comparisons and logical operations.
lessons.code:
is_raining = True
print(5 > 3)       # True
print(True + True) # 2

topics.title: Printing & Output
topics.description: Learn how to display text and values on the screen using Python's print() function.

lessons.title: The print() function
lessons.body: print() is the simplest way to show output. You can pass any value or variable to it and Python will display it in the console.
lessons.code:
print("Hello, world!")
print(42)
print(3.14)

lessons.title: Printing multiple values
lessons.body: You can pass several values separated by commas. By default, print() adds a space between them and a newline at the end.
lessons.code:
name = "Alice"
age = 30
print("Name:", name, "Age:", age)
# Name: Alice Age: 30

lessons.title: f-strings
lessons.body: f-strings (formatted string literals) let you embed expressions directly inside a string. Prefix the string with f and place expressions in curly braces.
lessons.code:
name = "Bob"
score = 95
print(f"{name} scored {score} points.")
# Bob scored 95 points.

lessons.title: sep and end parameters
lessons.body: Use sep to change the separator between values, and end to change what's printed after the last value (default is a newline).
lessons.code:
print("a", "b", "c", sep="-")   # a-b-c
print("loading", end="...")
print("done")                    # loading...done

topics.title: Comments & Code Readability
topics.description: Discover how to write comments in Python to make your code easier to understand.

lessons.title: What is a comment?
lessons.body: A comment is a note in your code that Python ignores when running the program. Comments help you and others understand what the code does.

lessons.title: Single-line comments
lessons.body: Use the # character to start a comment. Everything after # on that line is ignored by Python.
lessons.code:
# This is a comment
x = 10  # This sets x to 10
print(x)

lessons.title: Multi-line comments
lessons.body: Python doesn't have a special multi-line comment syntax, but you can use triple-quoted strings as an informal block comment when they aren't assigned to a variable.
lessons.code:
"""
This block describes what the program does.
It spans multiple lines.
"""
print("Program starts here")

lessons.title: When to write comments
lessons.body: Write comments to explain why you made a decision, not just what the code does. Clear variable names often reduce the need for comments — but complex logic always benefits from an explanation.

topics.title: User Input
topics.description: Learn how to read text entered by the user at runtime using the input() function.

lessons.title: The input() function
lessons.body: input() pauses the program and waits for the user to type something and press Enter. It always returns a string.
lessons.code:
name = input("What is your name? ")
print("Hello,", name)

lessons.title: Converting input to a number
lessons.body: Because input() returns a string, you must convert it before doing arithmetic. Use int() for whole numbers or float() for decimals.
lessons.code:
age = int(input("Enter your age: "))
print("In 10 years you will be", age + 10)

lessons.title: Handling invalid input
lessons.body: If the user types something that can't be converted, Python raises a ValueError. You'll learn to handle errors properly later — for now, trust that the user enters valid data.
lessons.code:
number = float(input("Enter a decimal: "))
print("Doubled:", number * 2)

topics.title: Arithmetic Operators
topics.description: Explore Python's built-in arithmetic operators for performing calculations.

lessons.title: Basic operations
lessons.body: Python supports the four classic operations: addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), and division (/). Division always produces a float.

lessons.title: Floor division and modulo
lessons.body: // performs floor division — it divides and rounds down to the nearest integer. % gives the remainder after division.

lessons.title: Exponentiation
lessons.body: Use ** to raise a number to a power. It works with integers and floats.

lessons.title: Operator precedence
lessons.body: Python follows standard math rules: exponentiation first, then multiplication/division/modulo, then addition/subtraction. Use parentheses to control evaluation order.

topics.title: Comparison & Logical Operators
topics.description: Use comparison and logical operators to evaluate conditions that return True or False.

lessons.title: Comparison operators
lessons.body: Comparison operators compare two values and return a boolean. The six operators are: == (equal), != (not equal), >, <, >=, <=.
lessons.code:
print(5 == 5)   # True
print(5 != 3)   # True
print(10 > 7)   # True
print(4 >= 4)   # True

lessons.title: Logical and / or / not
lessons.body: and returns True if both conditions are True. or returns True if at least one is True. not flips a boolean.
lessons.code:
age = 20
has_id = True
print(age >= 18 and has_id)  # True
print(age < 18 or has_id)    # True
print(not has_id)             # False

lessons.title: Chained comparisons
lessons.body: Python lets you chain comparisons naturally, like in mathematics. This is more readable than using and explicitly.
lessons.code:
x = 5
print(1 < x < 10)   # True
print(0 <= x <= 5)  # True

topics.title: Type Conversion
topics.description: Learn how to convert values between Python's built-in types using constructor functions.

lessons.title: Why convert types?
lessons.body: Sometimes a value is stored as the wrong type. For example, input() always returns a string, but you may need a number. Python's built-in functions let you convert between types.

lessons.title: int(), float(), str()
lessons.body: int() converts to an integer, float() to a floating-point number, and str() to a string. Conversion can fail if the value isn't compatible.
lessons.code:
print(int("42"))     # 42
print(float("3.14")) # 3.14
print(str(100))      # '100'

lessons.title: bool() and truthiness
lessons.body: bool() converts a value to True or False. Most values are truthy; exceptions include 0, 0.0, empty strings, and None.
lessons.code:
print(bool(0))     # False
print(bool(""))    # False
print(bool(42))    # True
print(bool("hi"))  # True

lessons.title: Implicit vs explicit conversion
lessons.body: Python sometimes converts types automatically (implicit), such as promoting an int to float in mixed arithmetic. Explicit conversion is when you call int(), float(), etc. yourself — always prefer explicit to avoid surprises.
lessons.code:
print(3 + 1.5)      # 4.5 (int promoted to float)
print(int(3 + 1.5)) # 4   (explicit truncation)

topics.title: Conditionals: if / elif / else
topics.description: Control the flow of your program by executing different code based on conditions.

lessons.title: The if statement
lessons.body: An if statement runs a block of code only when a condition is True. The block must be indented (4 spaces by convention).

lessons.title: else and elif
lessons.body: else runs when the if condition is False. elif (else-if) lets you check additional conditions in sequence. Only the first matching branch executes.

lessons.title: Nested conditions
lessons.body: You can place if statements inside other if statements to check multiple related conditions. Keep nesting shallow to maintain readability.

lessons.title: One-line conditionals
lessons.body: Python supports a ternary expression: value_if_true if condition else value_if_false. Use it for simple, short choices.
lessons.code:
age = 20
status = "adult" if age >= 18 else "minor"
print(status)  # adult

topics.title: For Loops
topics.description: Repeat actions over sequences of values using Python's for loop.

lessons.title: Iterating over a sequence
lessons.body: A for loop goes through each item in a sequence (like a list or string) one at a time. The loop variable takes the value of each item in turn.

lessons.title: Using range()
lessons.body: range(n) produces integers from 0 up to (but not including) n. range(start, stop, step) gives you more control.

lessons.title: Looping over strings
lessons.body: Strings are sequences too. A for loop visits each character one by one.

lessons.title: break and continue
lessons.body: break exits the loop immediately. continue skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next one.

topics.title: While Loops
topics.description: Repeat a block of code as long as a condition remains True using the while loop.

lessons.title: Basic while loop
lessons.body: A while loop checks a condition before every iteration. If the condition is True, it runs the body; otherwise it stops.
lessons.code:
count = 0
while count < 5:
    print(count)
    count += 1

lessons.title: Infinite loops and break
lessons.body: If the condition never becomes False, the loop runs forever (an infinite loop). Use break to exit when a certain event occurs.

lessons.title: while vs for
lessons.body: Use a for loop when you know in advance how many times to iterate (or you're iterating over a collection). Use while when the number of iterations depends on a condition that changes during the loop.

topics.title: Defining Functions
topics.description: Package reusable logic into functions using def, parameters, and return values.

lessons.title: What is a function?
lessons.body: A function is a named block of code that performs a specific task. You define it once and can call it as many times as you need, avoiding repetition.

lessons.title: def, parameters, and return
lessons.body: Use def to define a function. Parameters are the inputs; return sends a value back to the caller.
lessons.code:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(3, 4)
print(result)  # 7

lessons.title: Default parameter values
lessons.body: You can give parameters default values. If the caller doesn't supply an argument, the default is used instead.
lessons.code:
def greet(name, greeting="Hello"):
    return f"{greeting}, {name}!"

print(greet("Alice"))          # Hello, Alice!
print(greet("Bob", "Hi"))      # Hi, Bob!

lessons.title: Functions without a return value
lessons.body: A function that doesn't explicitly return a value implicitly returns None. These functions are useful for side effects like printing.
lessons.code:
def say_hello(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

result = say_hello("Charlie")
print(result)  # None

topics.title: Introduction to Lists
topics.description: Store multiple values in a single variable using Python lists.

lessons.title: Creating a list
lessons.body: A list is an ordered collection of items enclosed in square brackets. Items can be of any type, and a list can mix types.
lessons.code:
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
names = ["Alice", "Bob"]
mixed = [1, "hello", True]
print(numbers)

lessons.title: Accessing items by index
lessons.body: Each item has an index starting at 0. Negative indices count from the end: -1 is the last item.
lessons.code:
colors = ["red", "green", "blue"]
print(colors[0])   # red
print(colors[-1])  # blue

lessons.title: Adding and removing items
lessons.body: append() adds an item to the end. remove() deletes the first occurrence of a value.
lessons.code:
items = ["a", "b", "c"]
items.append("d")
print(items)         # ['a', 'b', 'c', 'd']
items.remove("b")
print(items)         # ['a', 'c', 'd']

lessons.title: Useful list functions
lessons.body: len() returns the number of items. sorted() returns a new sorted list. sum() adds up numeric items.
lessons.code:
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
print(len(nums))       # 5
print(sorted(nums))    # [1, 1, 3, 4, 5]
print(sum(nums))       # 14

topics.title: Indexing & Slicing
topics.description: Access specific elements or sub-sequences from strings and lists using indexing and slicing.

lessons.title: Zero-based indexing
lessons.body: Python counts positions from 0. The first element is at index 0, the second at 1, and so on. Negative indices count from the end.
lessons.code:
word = "Python"
print(word[0])   # P
print(word[-1])  # n

lessons.title: Slicing basics
lessons.body: A slice [start:stop] returns a portion of a sequence. start is inclusive, stop is exclusive. Omitting either defaults to the beginning or end.
lessons.code:
s = "Hello, World!"
print(s[0:5])   # Hello
print(s[7:])    # World!
print(s[:5])    # Hello

lessons.title: Step in slices
lessons.body: Adding a step [start:stop:step] skips elements. A step of -1 reverses the sequence.

topics.title: String Methods
topics.description: Manipulate and inspect text using Python's built-in string methods.

lessons.title: Case methods
lessons.body: upper() and lower() change the case of all characters. capitalize() uppercases only the first character.
lessons.code:
s = "hello WORLD"
print(s.upper())      # HELLO WORLD
print(s.lower())      # hello world
print(s.capitalize()) # Hello world

lessons.title: strip, split, join
lessons.body: strip() removes leading and trailing whitespace. split() breaks a string into a list. join() does the reverse — it combines a list of strings into one.
lessons.code:
text = "  hello  "
print(text.strip())              # 'hello'
words = "a,b,c".split(",")      # ['a', 'b', 'c']
print("-".join(words))           # a-b-c

lessons.title: Searching inside strings
lessons.body: find() returns the index of the first occurrence (-1 if not found). in is often simpler for a True/False check.
lessons.code:
msg = "Good morning!"
print(msg.find("morning"))  # 5
print("Good" in msg)        # True

lessons.title: replace()
lessons.body: replace(old, new) returns a new string with all occurrences of old replaced by new. Strings are immutable — replace() doesn't change the original.
lessons.code:
s = "I like cats and cats like me"
print(s.replace("cats", "dogs"))
# I like dogs and dogs like me

topics.title: None & the Absence of a Value
topics.description: Understand what None means in Python and how to check for it correctly.

lessons.title: What is None?
lessons.body: None is Python's way of representing 'nothing' or 'no value'. It is its own type (NoneType) and is the only value of that type.
lessons.code:
x = None
print(x)          # None
print(type(x))    # <class 'NoneType'>

lessons.title: Functions that return None
lessons.body: Functions that don't explicitly return a value return None automatically. Forgetting to return is a common source of bugs.
lessons.code:
def double(n):
    result = n * 2  # forgot return!

val = double(5)
print(val)  # None

lessons.title: Checking for None
lessons.body: Always use is None or is not None to check for None, not == None. The is operator checks identity, which is the correct semantics for singleton objects like None.

topics.title: Lists & List Methods
topics.description: Learn how to store, access, and manipulate ordered collections of items using Python lists.

lessons.title: Creating a list
lessons.body: A list is an ordered, mutable collection. Create one with square brackets.
lessons.code:
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
print(fruits[0])  # 'apple'

lessons.title: Common methods
lessons.body: append() adds to the end. remove() deletes the first matching value. pop() removes by index and returns the value.
lessons.code:
fruits.append("date")
fruits.remove("banana")
last = fruits.pop()

lessons.title: Slicing
lessons.body: Use [start:stop:step] to get a sub-list. Negative indices count from the end.

lessons.title: List comprehensions
lessons.body: A concise way to create a new list by transforming or filtering another sequence.
lessons.code:
squares = [x**2 for x in range(1, 6)]
# [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

topics.title: Tuples
topics.description: Store fixed, ordered collections of values using Python's immutable tuple type.

lessons.title: Creating a tuple
lessons.body: A tuple is like a list but immutable — you can't change its items after creation. Use parentheses or just commas.
lessons.code:
point = (3, 7)
colors = "red", "green", "blue"
print(point[0])  # 3

lessons.title: Tuple unpacking
lessons.body: You can unpack a tuple into individual variables in one line. The number of variables must match the number of elements.
lessons.code:
x, y = (10, 20)
first, *rest = (1, 2, 3, 4)
print(first)  # 1
print(rest)   # [2, 3, 4]

lessons.title: When to use tuples
lessons.body: Use tuples for data that should not change — coordinates, RGB values, function return values. They are also slightly faster than lists and can be used as dictionary keys.
lessons.code:
location = (48.8566, 2.3522)  # Paris lat/lon
rgb = (255, 165, 0)            # orange

lessons.title: Tuple methods
lessons.body: Tuples only have two methods: count() returns how many times a value appears, and index() returns the position of the first match.
lessons.code:
t = (1, 2, 2, 3, 2)
print(t.count(2))   # 3
print(t.index(3))   # 3

topics.title: Dictionaries
topics.description: Map keys to values for fast lookups using Python's built-in dictionary type.

lessons.title: Creating a dictionary
lessons.body: A dictionary stores key–value pairs. Keys must be unique and immutable. Create one with curly braces.
lessons.code:
person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30}
print(person["name"])  # Alice

lessons.title: Adding and updating entries
lessons.body: Assign to a key to add or overwrite it. Use del to remove an entry.
lessons.code:
person["city"] = "Paris"   # add
person["age"] = 31         # update
del person["city"]          # remove

lessons.title: Accessing safely with get()
lessons.body: Accessing a missing key raises a KeyError. Use get() to return a default value instead.
lessons.code:
score = {"Alice": 95}
print(score.get("Bob", 0))  # 0 (not KeyError)

lessons.title: Iterating over a dictionary
lessons.body: Use .keys(), .values(), or .items() to iterate. .items() gives you both at once.

topics.title: Sets
topics.description: Work with unordered collections of unique items using Python sets.

lessons.title: Creating a set
lessons.body: A set holds unique, unordered items. Use curly braces or set(). Duplicates are silently dropped.

lessons.title: add, remove, discard
lessons.body: add() inserts an element. remove() raises a KeyError if missing. discard() does nothing if the item isn't present.
lessons.code:
s = {1, 2, 3}
s.add(4)
s.discard(10)  # safe — no error
s.remove(2)

lessons.title: Set operations
lessons.body: Sets support union (|), intersection (&), difference (-), and symmetric difference (^) — just like in mathematics.

lessons.title: Membership testing
lessons.body: Checking if an item is in a set is O(1) — much faster than scanning a list for large collections.

topics.title: Function Arguments In Depth
topics.description: Master positional, keyword, *args, and **kwargs to write flexible Python functions.

lessons.title: Positional vs keyword arguments
lessons.body: Positional arguments are matched by order. Keyword arguments are passed by name and can appear in any order.
lessons.code:
def greet(first, last):
    print(f"{first} {last}")

greet("Alice", "Smith")           # positional
greet(last="Jones", first="Bob")  # keyword

lessons.title: *args — variable positional arguments
lessons.body: *args collects any number of extra positional arguments into a tuple. The name args is a convention — the * is what matters.

lessons.title: **kwargs — variable keyword arguments
lessons.body: **kwargs collects any number of extra keyword arguments into a dictionary.

lessons.title: Combining argument types
lessons.body: You can combine regular, *args, and **kwargs. The order must be: regular → *args → keyword-only → **kwargs.
lessons.code:
def fn(a, b, *args, key=0, **kwargs):
    pass

topics.title: Variable Scope
topics.description: Understand local, global, and enclosing scopes to avoid name-clash bugs in Python.

lessons.title: Local scope
lessons.body: Variables created inside a function are local — they only exist for the duration of that function call and are invisible outside.
lessons.code:
def fn():
    x = 10
    print(x)

fn()       # 10
# print(x) # NameError

lessons.title: Global scope
lessons.body: Variables defined at the top level of a module are global. Functions can read them without any declaration.
lessons.code:
greeting = "Hello"

def say():
    print(greeting)  # reads global

say()

lessons.title: The global keyword
lessons.body: If you need to assign to a global variable from inside a function, declare it with global first.
lessons.code:
count = 0

def increment():
    global count
    count += 1

increment()
print(count)  # 1

lessons.title: LEGB rule
lessons.body: Python looks up names in this order: Local → Enclosing (nested functions) → Global → Built-in. The first match wins.
lessons.code:
x = "global"

def outer():
    x = "enclosing"
    def inner():
        print(x)  # 'enclosing'
    inner()

outer()

topics.title: String Formatting
topics.description: Build and format strings effectively using f-strings, format(), and other techniques.

lessons.title: f-strings (recommended)
lessons.body: f-strings are the modern, readable way to embed variables in strings. Prefix with f and put expressions in {}.
lessons.code:
name = "Alice"
age = 30
print(f"My name is {name} and I am {age} years old.")

lessons.title: Format specifiers
lessons.body: Inside the braces of an f-string you can add a format spec after a colon to control precision, width, alignment, and more.

lessons.title: str.format()
lessons.body: str.format() is an older approach still widely used. Positional or named placeholders are filled in by the arguments.
lessons.code:
print("{} + {} = {}".format(1, 2, 3))
print("{name} is {age}".format(name="Bob", age=25))

lessons.title: % formatting (legacy)
lessons.body: The oldest form uses % as a placeholder. You'll encounter it in older code. Prefer f-strings for new code.
lessons.code: print("Hello, %s! You are %d years old." % ("Carol", 28))

topics.title: Exceptions & Error Handling
topics.description: Catch and handle runtime errors gracefully with try / except / finally.

lessons.title: What is an exception?
lessons.body: An exception is an error that occurs at runtime. If not handled, it stops your program and prints a traceback. Common examples: ZeroDivisionError, ValueError, TypeError.

lessons.title: try / except
lessons.body: Wrap risky code in a try block. If an exception is raised, the matching except block runs instead of crashing.

lessons.title: else and finally
lessons.body: else runs only when no exception occurred. finally always runs — useful for cleanup like closing files.

lessons.title: Raising exceptions
lessons.body: Use raise to trigger an exception intentionally — for example, to enforce preconditions in your functions.

topics.title: File I/O
topics.description: Read from and write to files using Python's built-in open() function.

lessons.title: Opening and reading a file
lessons.body: Use open() with mode 'r' to read. The with statement closes the file automatically even if an error occurs.
lessons.code:
with open("data.txt", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()
print(content)

lessons.title: Reading line by line
lessons.body: Iterating over the file object yields lines one at a time — memory-efficient for large files.

lessons.title: Writing to a file
lessons.body: Use mode 'w' to write (overwrites existing content) or 'a' to append. write() does not add a newline automatically.
lessons.code:
with open("output.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Hello\n")
    f.write("World\n")

lessons.title: File paths and encoding
lessons.body: Always specify encoding='utf-8' to avoid platform-specific surprises. Use pathlib.Path for portable path manipulation.
lessons.code:
from pathlib import Path

path = Path("data") / "notes.txt"
with open(path, encoding="utf-8") as f:
    print(f.read())

topics.title: Comprehensions
topics.description: Write concise, readable transformations of collections using list, dict, and set comprehensions.

lessons.title: List comprehensions
lessons.body: A list comprehension creates a new list by applying an expression to each item in an iterable, with an optional filter.
lessons.code:
evens = [x for x in range(20) if x % 2 == 0]
print(evens)  # [0, 2, 4, ..., 18]

lessons.title: Dict comprehensions
lessons.body: Dict comprehensions build a dictionary using {key: value for item in iterable} syntax.

lessons.title: Set comprehensions
lessons.body: Set comprehensions use curly braces like dict comprehensions but produce a set — duplicates are removed automatically.

lessons.title: Nested comprehensions
lessons.body: You can nest comprehensions to flatten or transform nested structures. Keep them short — if it's hard to read, use a loop.
lessons.code:
matrix = [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]]
flat = [n for row in matrix for n in row]
print(flat)  # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

topics.title: Modules & Imports
topics.description: Organise code across files and reuse the Python standard library through the import system.

lessons.title: Importing a module
lessons.body: import loads a module and binds it to a name. Use dot notation to access its attributes.
lessons.code:
import math
print(math.sqrt(16))   # 4.0
print(math.pi)         # 3.14159...

lessons.title: from … import
lessons.body: Import specific names from a module so you can use them without the module prefix.
lessons.code:
from math import sqrt, pi
print(sqrt(25))  # 5.0
print(pi)        # 3.14159...

lessons.title: Aliasing with as
lessons.body: Use as to give a module or name a shorter alias — common for libraries like numpy (np) or datetime (dt).
lessons.code:
import datetime as dt
now = dt.datetime.now()
print(now)

lessons.title: Creating your own module
lessons.body: Any .py file is a module. Put reusable functions there and import them from other scripts. The __name__ == '__main__' guard prevents code from running on import.

topics.title: Intro to Classes & Objects
topics.description: Model real-world entities with classes, attributes, and methods in Python's object-oriented style.

lessons.title: What is a class?
lessons.body: A class is a blueprint for creating objects. Each object (instance) has its own data (attributes) and behaviour (methods).

lessons.title: __init__ and self
lessons.body: __init__ is called automatically when an object is created. self refers to the instance being constructed.

lessons.title: Instance methods
lessons.body: Methods are functions defined inside a class. They always receive self as their first parameter.

lessons.title: __str__ and __repr__
lessons.body: __str__ defines the human-readable string representation (used by print). __repr__ is the unambiguous developer representation.

topics.title: Inheritance
topics.description: Reuse and extend class behaviour through single and multi-level inheritance in Python.

lessons.title: Subclassing
lessons.body: Put the parent class in parentheses to create a subclass. The child inherits all methods and attributes of the parent.

lessons.title: super()
lessons.body: super() gives access to the parent class. Use it in __init__ to call the parent's initialiser before adding child-specific setup.

lessons.title: isinstance() and issubclass()
lessons.body: isinstance(obj, cls) returns True if obj is an instance of cls or any subclass. issubclass(A, B) checks the class hierarchy.
lessons.code:
class A: pass
class B(A): pass

b = B()
print(isinstance(b, A))  # True
print(issubclass(B, A))  # True

lessons.title: Method resolution order (MRO)
lessons.body: Python uses the C3 linearisation to determine which method gets called first in a multiple-inheritance hierarchy. Use ClassName.__mro__ to inspect it.
lessons.code:
class X: pass
class Y(X): pass
class Z(X): pass
class W(Y, Z): pass
print(W.__mro__)

topics.title: Iterators & the for Loop Protocol
topics.description: Understand how Python's for loop works under the hood with iterables and iterators.

lessons.title: Iterables vs iterators
lessons.body: An iterable is anything you can loop over (list, string, range). An iterator is the object that tracks the current position and provides the next value.
lessons.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3]
it = iter(nums)      # get iterator
print(next(it))      # 1
print(next(it))      # 2

lessons.title: How for uses __iter__ and __next__
lessons.body: A for loop calls iter() on the iterable to get an iterator, then repeatedly calls next() until StopIteration is raised.

lessons.title: enumerate() and zip()
lessons.body: enumerate() pairs each item with its index. zip() combines multiple iterables element by element.

topics.title: Lambda Functions & Higher-Order Functions
topics.description: Write compact anonymous functions with lambda and apply them with map, filter, and sorted.

lessons.title: Lambda expressions
lessons.body: lambda creates a small anonymous function inline. It can take any number of arguments but can only contain a single expression.
lessons.code:
double = lambda x: x * 2
print(double(5))  # 10

add = lambda a, b: a + b
print(add(3, 4))  # 7

lessons.title: map() and filter()
lessons.body: map(fn, iterable) applies fn to every item. filter(fn, iterable) keeps only items where fn returns True. Both return lazy iterators — wrap in list() to see the results.
lessons.code:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
squared = list(map(lambda x: x**2, nums))
evens = list(filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, nums))
print(squared)  # [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]
print(evens)    # [2, 4]

lessons.title: sorted() with a key
lessons.body: sorted() accepts an optional key function that determines the sort order. Lambda is perfect here.
lessons.code:
words = ["banana", "apple", "kiwi"]
print(sorted(words, key=lambda w: len(w)))
# ['kiwi', 'apple', 'banana']

lessons.title: When to prefer def over lambda
lessons.body: Use lambda for short, throwaway functions. If the logic is complex or you'll reuse it, write a proper def for readability and debuggability.

topics.title: Decorators
topics.description: Understand how Python decorators wrap functions to add behaviour without modifying the original code.

lessons.title: Functions as first-class objects
lessons.body: In Python, functions are objects. You can pass them as arguments, return them, and assign them to variables.
lessons.code:
def greet():
    return "Hello"

say = greet
print(say())  # 'Hello'

lessons.title: Writing a decorator
lessons.body: A decorator is a function that takes another function and returns a new function with added behaviour.

lessons.title: Applying with @
lessons.body: The @ syntax is syntactic sugar for reassigning the function through the decorator.

lessons.title: functools.wraps
lessons.body: Use @functools.wraps(fn) inside your wrapper to preserve the original function's name and docstring.

topics.title: Generators
topics.description: Produce values lazily to save memory and enable infinite sequences using generator functions and expressions.

lessons.title: yield vs return
lessons.body: A generator function uses yield to emit values one at a time, pausing execution between calls. It returns a generator object rather than a single value.

lessons.title: Generator expressions
lessons.body: Generator expressions look like list comprehensions but use parentheses. They are lazy — values are computed only when requested.
lessons.code:
squares = (x**2 for x in range(1_000_000))
print(next(squares))  # 0
print(next(squares))  # 1

lessons.title: Memory advantage
lessons.body: Generators don't store all values in memory at once — ideal for processing large files or streams line by line.

lessons.title: send() and two-way communication
lessons.body: Generators can receive values back via send(). This turns them into coroutine-like objects useful for pipelines.
lessons.code:
def accumulator():
    total = 0
    while True:
        n = yield total
        if n is None:
            break
        total += n

acc = accumulator()
next(acc)         # prime
print(acc.send(10))  # 10
print(acc.send(5))   # 15

topics.title: Context Managers
topics.description: Manage resources cleanly using the with statement and the context manager protocol.

lessons.title: The with statement
lessons.body: with ensures setup and teardown code runs reliably — even if an exception occurs. Files, locks, and database connections are common use cases.
lessons.code:
with open("data.txt") as f:
    data = f.read()
# file is closed here automatically

lessons.title: __enter__ and __exit__
lessons.body: Any class implementing __enter__ and __exit__ can be used with the with statement. __exit__ receives exception info if one was raised.

lessons.title: contextlib.contextmanager
lessons.body: The @contextmanager decorator lets you write a context manager as a generator function — much simpler than a full class.
lessons.code:
from contextlib import contextmanager

@contextmanager
def managed():
    print("enter")
    yield
    print("exit")

with managed():
    print("inside")

topics.title: Closures
topics.description: Create functions that capture and remember variables from their enclosing scope.

lessons.title: What is a closure?
lessons.body: A closure is an inner function that captures (closes over) variables from its enclosing function. The captured variables persist even after the outer function has returned.

lessons.title: The cell object
lessons.body: Python stores captured variables in 'cell' objects accessible via __closure__. This is an implementation detail but useful for debugging.

lessons.title: nonlocal keyword
lessons.body: To assign to a captured variable (not just read it), declare it with nonlocal inside the inner function.
lessons.code:
def make_counter():
    count = 0
    def increment():
        nonlocal count
        count += 1
        return count
    return increment

counter = make_counter()
print(counter())  # 1
print(counter())  # 2

lessons.title: Closures vs classes
lessons.body: Closures and classes both encapsulate state. Prefer closures for lightweight, single-method objects. Use classes when behaviour grows more complex.

topics.title: Type Hints
topics.description: Add optional static type annotations to make your code self-documenting and compatible with type checkers.

lessons.title: Basic annotations
lessons.body: Python's type hints are optional and do not affect runtime behaviour. They serve as documentation and enable static analysis tools like mypy.

lessons.title: Collections and generics
lessons.body: Use list[int], dict[str, int], tuple[str, ...] etc. to annotate collection types (Python 3.9+). Use typing module for older versions.

lessons.title: Optional and Union
lessons.body: Optional[T] means the value can be T or None. Union[A, B] means it can be A or B. In Python 3.10+ you can write T | None and A | B.

lessons.title: Callable and TypeVar
lessons.body: Callable[[ArgTypes], ReturnType] annotates function parameters. TypeVar enables generic functions that work with many types while preserving type safety.

topics.title: Dataclasses
topics.description: Reduce boilerplate when creating data-holding classes using the @dataclass decorator.

lessons.title: Basic @dataclass
lessons.body: The @dataclass decorator auto-generates __init__, __repr__, and __eq__ from your field annotations.
lessons.code:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Point:
    x: float
    y: float

p = Point(1.0, 2.5)
print(p)  # Point(x=1.0, y=2.5)

lessons.title: Default values and field()
lessons.body: Simple defaults can be set inline. For mutable defaults like lists, use field(default_factory=...).

lessons.title: frozen=True for immutability
lessons.body: Use frozen=True to make instances immutable. This also makes them hashable so they can be used in sets or as dict keys.

lessons.title: __post_init__ for validation
lessons.body: __post_init__ runs after the generated __init__. Use it to validate fields or compute derived attributes.

topics.title: itertools
topics.description: Compose efficient iterators for combinations, permutations, grouping, and more using itertools.

lessons.title: chain and islice
lessons.body: chain() concatenates iterables without creating a new list. islice() slices any iterator lazily.
lessons.code:
from itertools import chain, islice

result = list(chain([1, 2], [3, 4], [5]))
print(result)  # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

print(list(islice(range(100), 5)))  # [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]

lessons.title: combinations and permutations
lessons.body: combinations() produces all r-length subsets (order doesn't matter). permutations() produces all ordered arrangements.
lessons.code:
from itertools import combinations, permutations

print(list(combinations("ABC", 2)))
# [('A','B'), ('A','C'), ('B','C')]

print(list(permutations("AB")))
# [('A','B'), ('B','A')]

lessons.title: groupby
lessons.body: groupby() groups consecutive elements that share the same key. Data must be sorted by the key first.
lessons.code:
from itertools import groupby

data = [("fruit", "apple"), ("fruit", "banana"), ("veg", "carrot")]
for key, group in groupby(data, key=lambda x: x[0]):
    print(key, [item[1] for item in group])

lessons.title: cycle and repeat
lessons.body: cycle() loops over an iterable forever. repeat() emits the same value n times (or forever). Both are lazy.
lessons.code:
from itertools import cycle, repeat

colors = cycle(["red", "green", "blue"])
for _ in range(5):
    print(next(colors))

print(list(repeat(0, 4)))  # [0, 0, 0, 0]

topics.title: Custom Exceptions & Exception Chaining
topics.description: Design your own exception hierarchy and chain exceptions to preserve error context.

lessons.title: Creating custom exceptions
lessons.body: Subclass Exception (or a more specific built-in) to create domain-specific exceptions. Add extra attributes for richer context.

lessons.title: raise … from
lessons.body: raise X from Y chains exceptions, preserving the original cause. The traceback shows both errors. raise X from None suppresses the chain.

lessons.title: Exception groups (Python 3.11+)
lessons.body: ExceptionGroup lets you raise and handle multiple unrelated exceptions at once — especially useful in concurrent code.

topics.title: Abstract Base Classes
topics.description: Define interfaces and enforce method implementation contracts using Python's abc module.

lessons.title: The abc module
lessons.body: ABC (Abstract Base Class) lets you define methods that subclasses must implement. Attempting to instantiate an incomplete subclass raises TypeError.

lessons.title: Implementing the interface
lessons.body: A concrete subclass must implement every abstract method. Only then can it be instantiated.

lessons.title: Abstract properties
lessons.body: Use @property together with @abstractmethod to require subclasses to expose a specific property.

lessons.title: Virtual subclasses
lessons.body: register() lets you declare that an existing class satisfies an ABC without modifying it — useful for third-party types.
lessons.code:
from abc import ABC

class Drawable(ABC):
    pass

Drawable.register(tuple)
print(isinstance((1, 2), Drawable))  # True

topics.title: Properties & Descriptors
topics.description: Control attribute access with @property, setters, and the descriptor protocol.

lessons.title: @property
lessons.body: Decorate a method with @property to access it like an attribute. This lets you add logic (e.g. validation) without breaking the public interface.

lessons.title: Setter and deleter
lessons.body: Add a @<name>.setter to allow assignment, and @<name>.deleter for deletion. Each receives the property name as the decorator.

lessons.title: The descriptor protocol
lessons.body: Any class implementing __get__, __set__, or __delete__ is a descriptor. When assigned to a class attribute, it intercepts all access from instances — this is how property works internally.

topics.title: Regular Expressions
topics.description: Search, match, and transform text using Python's re module and regex patterns.

lessons.title: re.search vs re.match
lessons.body: re.match() anchors at the start of the string. re.search() scans the entire string. Both return a Match object or None.
lessons.code:
import re

print(re.match(r"\d+", "42abc"))     # Match
print(re.search(r"\d+", "abc42xyz"))  # Match

lessons.title: Groups and capturing
lessons.body: Wrap part of a pattern in () to create a capturing group. Retrieve matches with .group() or .groups().
lessons.code:
m = re.search(r"(\w+)@(\w+\.\w+)", "alice@example.com")
if m:
    print(m.group(1))   # alice
    print(m.group(2))   # example.com

lessons.title: re.findall and re.sub
lessons.body: findall() returns all non-overlapping matches as a list. sub() replaces matches with a string or function.
lessons.code:
text = "call 555-1234 or 555-5678"
numbers = re.findall(r"\d{3}-\d{4}", text)
print(numbers)  # ['555-1234', '555-5678']

cleaned = re.sub(r"\d", "#", text)
print(cleaned)

lessons.title: Compiled patterns
lessons.body: Compile a pattern with re.compile() when you'll reuse it many times. This is faster and lets you store named groups cleanly.
lessons.code:
pattern = re.compile(r"(?P<year>\d{4})-(?P<month>\d{2})-(?P<day>\d{2})")
m = pattern.match("2024-07-15")
print(m.group("year"))   # 2024

topics.title: functools
topics.description: Boost code quality and performance with the utility functions in Python's functools module.

lessons.title: partial()
lessons.body: functools.partial() creates a new callable with some arguments pre-filled. Useful for adapting functions to callback APIs.
lessons.code:
from functools import partial

def power(base, exp):
    return base ** exp

square = partial(power, exp=2)
cube   = partial(power, exp=3)
print(square(5))  # 25
print(cube(3))    # 27

lessons.title: lru_cache()
lessons.body: lru_cache() memoises the return values of a function. Repeated calls with the same arguments hit the cache instead of recomputing.

lessons.title: reduce()
lessons.body: reduce() applies a function cumulatively to a sequence, reducing it to a single value.
lessons.code:
from functools import reduce

product = reduce(lambda a, b: a * b, [1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
print(product)  # 120

lessons.title: total_ordering
lessons.body: @total_ordering fills in the missing comparison methods (__lt__, __gt__, etc.) when you define __eq__ and one other comparison — less boilerplate in custom classes.

topics.title: pathlib
topics.description: Manipulate filesystem paths in an object-oriented, cross-platform way using pathlib.Path.

lessons.title: Creating and joining paths
lessons.body: Path objects represent file system paths. Use / to join segments — more readable than os.path.join.
lessons.code:
from pathlib import Path

base = Path("/home/user")
config = base / ".config" / "app.json"
print(config)

lessons.title: Reading and writing
lessons.body: read_text() and write_text() handle encoding automatically and don't require an explicit open().
lessons.code:
p = Path("notes.txt")
p.write_text("hello\n", encoding="utf-8")
content = p.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
print(content)

lessons.title: Querying path properties
lessons.body: Path objects expose .name, .stem, .suffix, .parent and methods like .exists(), .is_file(), .is_dir().
lessons.code:
p = Path("/data/report.csv")
print(p.name)     # report.csv
print(p.stem)     # report
print(p.suffix)   # .csv
print(p.parent)   # /data

lessons.title: Globbing and iteration
lessons.body: iterdir() lists a directory's contents. glob() and rglob() find files matching a pattern recursively.

topics.title: Threading
topics.description: Run I/O-bound tasks concurrently using Python's threading module and the GIL's implications.

lessons.title: Creating and starting a thread
lessons.body: Thread(target=fn) creates a thread that will run fn. Call start() to launch it and join() to wait for it to finish.
lessons.code:
import threading

def worker(n):
    print(f"Worker {n}")

t = threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(1,))
t.start()
t.join()

lessons.title: The Global Interpreter Lock (GIL)
lessons.body: The GIL limits Python to executing one thread at a time for CPU-bound tasks. For I/O-bound work (network, files), threads still provide real concurrency because they release the GIL while waiting.

lessons.title: Locks and race conditions
lessons.body: When multiple threads share mutable state, use a Lock to prevent data corruption. Acquire the lock before the critical section and release it after.
lessons.code:
import threading

counter = 0
lock = threading.Lock()

def increment():
    global counter
    with lock:
        counter += 1

lessons.title: ThreadPoolExecutor
lessons.body: concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor manages a pool of threads. submit() schedules a call and returns a Future object.
lessons.code:
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

def fetch(url):
    return f"data from {url}"

with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) as ex:
    futures = [ex.submit(fetch, u) for u in ["a", "b", "c"]]
    results = [f.result() for f in futures]

topics.title: Logging
topics.description: Replace print() with Python's logging module for structured, configurable, and production-ready diagnostics.

lessons.title: Logging levels
lessons.body: Python's logging module defines five severity levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, and CRITICAL. Only messages at or above the configured level are emitted.
lessons.code:
import logging

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
logging.debug("Verbose detail")
logging.info("Normal operation")
logging.warning("Something unexpected")
logging.error("A failure occurred")

lessons.title: Named loggers
lessons.body: Create a logger per module using getLogger(__name__). Loggers form a hierarchy: setting the root logger's level affects all children.
lessons.code:
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
logger.info("Module-level message")

lessons.title: Handlers and formatters
lessons.body: Handlers route log records to destinations (console, file, HTTP). Formatters control the output structure.
lessons.code:
import logging

handler = logging.FileHandler("app.log")
formatter = logging.Formatter("%(asctime)s %(levelname)s %(message)s")
handler.setFormatter(formatter)

logger = logging.getLogger("myapp")
logger.addHandler(handler)
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

lessons.title: Structured logging
lessons.body: Pass extra context as keyword arguments or use a JSON formatter for machine-readable logs. Avoid f-strings in log calls — pass args separately so filtering can skip string formatting entirely.
lessons.code: logger.info("User %s logged in from %s", user_id, ip_address)

topics.title: AsyncIO & Coroutines
topics.description: Master Python's asyncio framework for writing concurrent I/O-bound code using coroutines.

lessons.title: The event loop
lessons.body: asyncio runs an event loop that schedules coroutines. The loop runs until all coroutines complete. asyncio.run() is the entry point in modern Python.

lessons.title: async def and await
lessons.body: Coroutines are defined with async def. Use await to pause execution until an awaitable completes.
lessons.code:
import asyncio

async def fetch(url: str) -> str:
    await asyncio.sleep(1)  # simulates I/O
    return f"data from {url}"

lessons.title: Running concurrently with gather
lessons.body: asyncio.gather() runs multiple coroutines concurrently. They interleave during I/O waits.
lessons.code:
async def main():
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        fetch("https://a.example"),
        fetch("https://b.example"),
    )
    print(results)

asyncio.run(main())

lessons.title: Tasks and cancellation
lessons.body: asyncio.create_task() schedules a coroutine immediately. You can cancel a task at any time.
lessons.code:
task = asyncio.create_task(fetch("https://c.example"))
await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
task.cancel()

topics.title: Metaclasses
topics.description: Control class creation itself by writing metaclasses — classes whose instances are other classes.

lessons.title: type is the default metaclass
lessons.body: Every class in Python is an instance of type. type(name, bases, namespace) is the low-level API for creating classes dynamically.
lessons.code:
MyClass = type("MyClass", (object,), {"x": 42})
print(MyClass.x)           # 42
print(type(MyClass))       # <class 'type'>

lessons.title: Writing a metaclass
lessons.body: Subclass type and override __new__ or __init__ to intercept class creation. Pass it via metaclass= in the class definition.

lessons.title: __init_subclass__
lessons.body: __init_subclass__ is a simpler hook called whenever a subclass is created. It avoids the boilerplate of a full metaclass for many common patterns.
lessons.code:
class Plugin:
    registry = []
    def __init_subclass__(cls, **kw):
        super().__init_subclass__(**kw)
        Plugin.registry.append(cls)

class AudioPlugin(Plugin): pass
print(Plugin.registry)  # [<class 'AudioPlugin'>]

lessons.title: class decorators vs metaclasses
lessons.body: Class decorators (applied with @) run after the class is created and are simpler to reason about. Prefer them over metaclasses unless you need to intercept class creation itself.

topics.title: Advanced Descriptors
topics.description: Build reusable attribute-management logic with data and non-data descriptors.

lessons.title: Data vs non-data descriptors
lessons.body: A data descriptor defines both __get__ and __set__ (or __delete__). A non-data descriptor defines only __get__. Data descriptors take priority over instance __dict__.

lessons.title: __set_name__
lessons.body: __set_name__(owner, name) is called at class creation and tells the descriptor which attribute name it was assigned to — no need to pass the name manually.

lessons.title: Building a validator descriptor
lessons.body: Descriptors shine when the same validation logic is needed on multiple attributes across multiple classes.

lessons.title: How functions become methods
lessons.body: Functions are non-data descriptors. When accessed on an instance, __get__ returns a bound method that prepends self automatically.
lessons.code:
class Foo:
    def bar(self): pass

print(type(Foo.__dict__["bar"]))  # function
print(type(Foo().bar))            # method

topics.title: Memory Management & Garbage Collection
topics.description: Understand Python's reference counting, cyclic garbage collector, and strategies to avoid memory leaks.

lessons.title: Reference counting
lessons.body: Python tracks how many references point to each object. When the count drops to zero, the memory is freed immediately — no GC pause needed for simple cases.
lessons.code:
import sys

x = [1, 2, 3]
print(sys.getrefcount(x))  # 2 (x + getrefcount arg)
y = x
print(sys.getrefcount(x))  # 3

lessons.title: Cyclic garbage collector
lessons.body: Reference cycles (A → B → A) prevent counts from reaching zero. Python's cyclic GC detects and collects such cycles. You can trigger it or configure thresholds with the gc module.
lessons.code:
import gc

class Node:
    def __init__(self):
        self.next = None

a = Node(); b = Node()
a.next = b; b.next = a
del a, b
gc.collect()  # free the cycle

lessons.title: weakref
lessons.body: A weak reference points to an object without incrementing its reference count. Useful for caches and observers that should not keep objects alive.
lessons.code:
import weakref

class Heavy:
    pass

obj = Heavy()
ref = weakref.ref(obj)
print(ref())   # <Heavy ...>
del obj
print(ref())   # None

lessons.title: __slots__ for memory savings
lessons.body: By default, each instance has a __dict__ (a hash map). Declaring __slots__ removes it, saving memory when you create thousands of instances.

topics.title: Structural Subtyping with Protocols
topics.description: Define structural interfaces using typing.Protocol for duck-typing that works with static type checkers.

lessons.title: What is a Protocol?
lessons.body: typing.Protocol defines a structural interface. Any class that has the right methods and attributes satisfies it — no explicit inheritance required (duck typing + static analysis).

lessons.title: Implicit implementation
lessons.body: A class satisfies a Protocol simply by having the required attributes — no inheritance or registration needed.

lessons.title: Runtime checkable protocols
lessons.body: Annotate a Protocol with @runtime_checkable to allow isinstance() checks at runtime.

lessons.title: Protocols vs ABCs
lessons.body: ABCs require explicit inheritance and support runtime polymorphism. Protocols are purely structural and work best with static type checkers. Use Protocols for library public APIs; ABCs for internal hierarchies that enforce contracts.

topics.title: Multiprocessing
topics.description: Bypass the GIL and use multiple CPU cores for CPU-bound tasks with Python's multiprocessing module.

lessons.title: Process vs Thread
lessons.body: A Process runs in a separate interpreter with its own memory space. Unlike threads, processes are not constrained by the GIL, so they can truly run in parallel on multiple cores.

lessons.title: Pool.map()
lessons.body: Pool distributes work across a pool of worker processes. map() applies a function to each item in a list and collects the results.

lessons.title: Shared state with Manager
lessons.body: Processes can't share regular Python objects. Use multiprocessing.Manager to create proxy objects (lists, dicts) that synchronise across processes.
lessons.code:
from multiprocessing import Process, Manager

def append_one(lst):
    lst.append(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    with Manager() as m:
        shared = m.list()
        p = Process(target=append_one, args=(shared,))
        p.start(); p.join()
        print(list(shared))  # [1]

lessons.title: ProcessPoolExecutor
lessons.body: concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor is the high-level API. Its interface mirrors ThreadPoolExecutor, making it easy to switch between threads and processes.
lessons.code:
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor

def cube(n):
    return n ** 3

if __name__ == "__main__":
    with ProcessPoolExecutor() as ex:
        results = list(ex.map(cube, range(6)))
        print(results)

topics.title: C Extensions & ctypes
topics.description: Call C libraries and write performance-critical code in C that integrates seamlessly with Python.

lessons.title: ctypes basics
lessons.body: ctypes lets you call functions in shared libraries (.so / .dll) directly from Python without writing any C extension code.
lessons.code:
import ctypes

libc = ctypes.CDLL("libc.so.6")
libc.printf(b"Hello from C!\n")

lessons.title: Defining argument and return types
lessons.body: Set argtypes and restype on a ctypes function to prevent silent type errors and enable automatic marshalling.
lessons.code:
import ctypes
math = ctypes.CDLL("libm.so.6")
math.sqrt.argtypes = [ctypes.c_double]
math.sqrt.restype  = ctypes.c_double
print(math.sqrt(2.0))

lessons.title: ctypes structures
lessons.body: ctypes.Structure lets you define C-compatible structs. Define _fields_ as a list of (name, ctype) pairs.
lessons.code:
import ctypes

class Point(ctypes.Structure):
    _fields_ = [("x", ctypes.c_float),
                ("y", ctypes.c_float)]

p = Point(1.0, 2.0)
print(p.x, p.y)

lessons.title: When to write a C extension
lessons.body: ctypes works for existing C libraries. For new performance-critical code, consider Cython (compiles Python-like syntax to C), cffi, or the CPython C API. Profile first — often NumPy or other libraries remove the need entirely.

topics.title: The ast Module
topics.description: Parse, inspect, and transform Python source code programmatically using the Abstract Syntax Tree.

lessons.title: Parsing source code
lessons.body: ast.parse() converts Python source code into a tree of AST nodes. You can then walk, inspect, or modify the tree.
lessons.code:
import ast

tree = ast.parse("x = 1 + 2")
print(ast.dump(tree, indent=2))

lessons.title: Walking the tree
lessons.body: ast.walk() yields every node in the tree in breadth-first order. Useful for collecting all names, calls, or literals.

lessons.title: NodeTransformer
lessons.body: Subclass ast.NodeTransformer and override visit_<NodeType> methods to return modified nodes. compile() then re-compiles the modified tree.

lessons.title: Use cases
lessons.body: The ast module powers linters (flake8, pylint), formatters (black), type checkers (mypy), and code coverage tools. You can also use it to build domain-specific languages embedded in Python.

topics.title: Import System & importlib
topics.description: Understand Python's import machinery and use importlib to load modules dynamically.

lessons.title: How import works
lessons.body: When you import a module Python checks sys.modules first (cache), then searches sys.path. Finders locate the module; loaders execute it and cache it.

lessons.title: importlib.import_module()
lessons.body: import_module() loads a module by name at runtime — useful for plugin systems where module names are only known at runtime.
lessons.code:
import importlib

mod = importlib.import_module("json")
print(mod.dumps({"a": 1}))

lessons.title: Custom finders and loaders
lessons.body: Implement importlib.abc.MetaPathFinder and Loader to intercept imports — e.g. to load modules from a database or a custom archive format.

lessons.title: Reloading modules
lessons.body: importlib.reload() re-executes a module's code in place. Useful during interactive development, but be aware that references held in other modules still point to the old objects.
lessons.code:
import importlib
import mymodule

importlib.reload(mymodule)

topics.title: Caching Strategies
topics.description: Apply memoisation and cache-aware patterns to eliminate redundant computation in Python programs.

lessons.title: functools.cache and lru_cache
lessons.body: functools.cache (Python 3.9+) is an unbounded cache. lru_cache(maxsize=N) evicts least-recently-used entries when full.

lessons.title: cached_property
lessons.body: functools.cached_property evaluates a property once and caches the result in the instance __dict__. Subsequent accesses skip the method entirely.

lessons.title: Manual TTL cache
lessons.body: When you need time-based expiry, implement a small cache class or use a third-party library. Store the result alongside the timestamp and recompute when expired.

lessons.title: Cache invalidation
lessons.body: Caches can return stale data. Always consider when to invalidate. For lru_cache, call fn.cache_clear(). For cached_property, delete the attribute from the instance __dict__.
lessons.code:
fib.cache_clear()

c = Circle(5)
del c.__dict__["area"]  # force recompute

topics.title: Profiling & Performance
topics.description: Measure, profile, and optimise Python programs using timeit, cProfile, and memory_profiler.

lessons.title: timeit for micro-benchmarks
lessons.body: timeit measures the execution time of a small snippet. It runs it many times to reduce noise.
lessons.code:
import timeit

t = timeit.timeit("[x**2 for x in range(1000)]", number=10_000)
print(f"{t:.3f} s")

lessons.title: cProfile for function-level profiling
lessons.body: cProfile counts calls and measures cumulative time per function. Run it from the CLI or programmatically.
lessons.code:
import cProfile

def slow():
    total = 0
    for i in range(10**6):
        total += i
    return total

cProfile.run("slow()")

lessons.title: line_profiler and memory_profiler
lessons.body: line_profiler (pip install line_profiler) profiles code line by line. memory_profiler tracks memory usage line by line. Both use a @profile decorator.

lessons.title: Common optimisation tips
lessons.body: Profile before optimising. Common wins: use built-ins (sum, min, max) instead of loops, prefer local variables over globals in tight loops, use NumPy for numerical work, and cache expensive calls.

topics.title: Advanced Testing with pytest
topics.description: Write robust test suites using pytest fixtures, parametrize, mocking, and property-based testing.

lessons.title: Fixtures
lessons.body: Fixtures provide reusable setup and teardown. pytest injects them by name into test functions. Use scope='session' to run a fixture once per test session.

lessons.title: @pytest.mark.parametrize
lessons.body: Run the same test with multiple inputs by parametrising it. Each (input, expected) pair becomes a separate test case.

lessons.title: Mocking with unittest.mock
lessons.body: Mock objects replace real dependencies in tests. patch() temporarily replaces an object in the module under test.
lessons.code:
from unittest.mock import patch

def test_send_email():
    with patch("myapp.smtp.send") as mock_send:
        send_welcome_email("alice@example.com")
        mock_send.assert_called_once()

lessons.title: Property-based testing with Hypothesis
lessons.body: Hypothesis generates random test cases that satisfy given strategies. It finds edge cases you wouldn't think to write manually.

topics.title: Concurrency Patterns
topics.description: Choose and apply the right concurrency model — threads, processes, or async — for each class of problem.

lessons.title: I/O-bound vs CPU-bound
lessons.body: I/O-bound tasks spend most of their time waiting (network, disk). CPU-bound tasks keep the processor busy. The right concurrency model depends on this distinction: asyncio or threads for I/O, multiprocessing for CPU.

lessons.title: asyncio with semaphores
lessons.body: asyncio.Semaphore limits the number of coroutines running concurrently — useful for rate-limiting outbound requests.
lessons.code:
import asyncio

sem = asyncio.Semaphore(5)

async def fetch(url):
    async with sem:
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)  # simulate request
        return url

lessons.title: Producer–consumer with asyncio.Queue
lessons.body: asyncio.Queue decouples producers that generate work from consumers that process it, enabling back-pressure.
lessons.code:
import asyncio

async def producer(q):
    for i in range(5):
        await q.put(i)

async def consumer(q):
    while True:
        item = await q.get()
        print("got", item)
        q.task_done()

async def main():
    q = asyncio.Queue()
    await asyncio.gather(producer(q), consumer(q))

lessons.title: Choosing the right tool
lessons.body: Use asyncio for many concurrent I/O tasks with low overhead. Use ThreadPoolExecutor to run blocking I/O in threads without rewriting code. Use ProcessPoolExecutor for CPU-intensive tasks that need true parallelism.

topics.title: Dunder Methods Deep Dive
topics.description: Implement Python's data model to make your objects behave like built-ins — supporting arithmetic, comparisons, containers, and more.

lessons.title: Arithmetic operators
lessons.body: Implement __add__, __sub__, __mul__, __truediv__ etc. to support arithmetic on custom objects. Return NotImplemented when the operation isn't supported to allow the other operand a chance.

lessons.title: Container protocol
lessons.body: Implement __len__, __getitem__, __setitem__, __contains__, and __iter__ to make your objects behave like built-in containers.

lessons.title: Callable objects
lessons.body: Implement __call__ to make an instance callable like a function. Useful for stateful function-like objects.

lessons.title: Context manager protocol
lessons.body: Implement __enter__ and __exit__ to support the with statement. __exit__ receives exception info and can suppress it by returning True.

topics.title: Advanced Type System
topics.description: Use TypeVar, ParamSpec, overload, and Literal to express complex type relationships that guide static analysis.

lessons.title: TypeVar and generic functions
lessons.body: TypeVar creates a placeholder for a type that is determined at call time. Generic functions preserve the relationship between input and output types.
lessons.code:
from typing import TypeVar

T = TypeVar("T")

def first(lst: list[T]) -> T:
    return lst[0]

x: int = first([1, 2, 3])   # T inferred as int
s: str = first(["a", "b"])  # T inferred as str

lessons.title: Generic classes
lessons.body: Subclass Generic[T] to create parameterisable classes, similar to list[int] or dict[str, int].

lessons.title: Literal and overload
lessons.body: Literal restricts a type to a fixed set of values. @overload lets you provide different signatures for the same function, enabling precise return-type inference based on argument values.

lessons.title: ParamSpec and Concatenate
lessons.body: ParamSpec captures the parameter specification of a callable, enabling type-safe decorators that preserve the wrapped function's signature.
